# Hestia Foundation — Workshop Curriculum
# 10-Day Self-Build Training Programme

**Version:** 1.0  
**Date:** 2026-05-29  
**Target audience:** Non-specialist volunteers (no construction experience required)  
**Outcome:** Participants can build a weathertight RCP dwelling shell  
**License:** CC BY-SA 4.0

---

## Programme Overview

| Day | Theme | Key Skills | Output |
|-----|-------|-----------|--------|
| 0 | Arrival & Setup | Site safety, tool familiarisation | Team ready |
| 1 | Foundation | Earth screws, levelling, floor joists | Floor complete |
| 2 | Panel Fabrication | Cutting, gluing, nailing, squaring | 4 panels ready |
| 3 | Panel Fabrication & Erection | OSB sheathing, wall erection, bracing | Walls standing |
| 4 | Envelope | Windows, doors, insulation, membranes | Weathertight walls |
| 5 | Roof | Roof joists, decking, membrane, gutters | Weathertight roof |
| 6 | Services | Solar PV, battery, hot water, LED lighting | Systems operational |
| 7 | Interior | Flooring, kitchen, paint, hardware | Interior complete |
| 8 | Exterior | Pine tar, trim, shutters, testing | Exterior finished |
| 9 | Commissioning | System testing, sensors, documentation | Handover ready |
| 10 | Buffer | Corrections, quality control, celebration | Complete |

---

## Learning Objectives

By the end of the workshop, participants will be able to:

1. **Understand** the RCP system principles and why it works
2. **Fabricate** a structural wall panel from I-joists, pallets, and OSB
3. **Erect** wall panels safely and squarely
4. **Install** a weathertight roof with membrane and gutters
5. **Fit** windows, doors, and insulation to achieve thermal performance
6. **Commission** basic off-grid electrical and hot water systems
7. **Apply** natural wood finishes for weather protection
8. **Document** their work for future replication

---

## Pre-Workshop Requirements

### For Participants
- Physical fitness: able to lift 20kg and work 8 hours/day
- No construction experience required
- Comfortable working at height (with fall protection)
- Enthusiasm for hands-on learning and teamwork

### Provided by Hestia Foundation
- All tools and safety equipment
- All materials (pre-cut where possible)
- Accommodation (camping or local lodging)
- Meals (communal cooking)
- Safety briefing and supervision
- Certificate of completion

---

## Day-by-Day Curriculum

### DAY 0 — Arrival and Setup
**Morning (08:00–12:00)**
- **Welcome circle:** Introductions, programme overview, expectations
- **Safety briefing:** Hard hats, glasses, dust masks, boots, gloves — mandatory rules
- **Site tour:** Walk the building footprint, understand the finished vision
- **Tool introduction:** Name, purpose, and safe operation of each tool
  - Cordless drill/driver — clutch settings, battery management
  - Circular saw — blade guard, cutting line, kickback prevention
  - Jigsaw — curves and openings
  - Laser level — setup, self-levelling, line projection
  - Impact driver — high-torque fastening
- **Practice session:** Cut scrap timber, drill holes, drive screws

**Afternoon (13:00–17:00)**
- **Material inspection:** Identify each material, understand its role
  - I-joists: why engineered timber is stronger than solid timber
  - EPAL pallets: how industrial waste becomes structure
  - OSB: why oriented strand board works for sheathing
  - Earth screws: why no concrete is needed
- **Team assignments:** Fabricators, installers, general support, documenter
- **Site preparation:** Mark building footprint, set up cutting station
- **Documentation briefing:** Daily photo/video requirements

**Day 0 Learning Check:**
- [ ] Can name all tools and describe safe use
- [ ] Can identify all materials and their structural role
- [ ] Understands personal protective equipment requirements
- [ ] Knows their team role and daily reporting responsibilities

---

### DAY 1 — Foundation and Floor Structure
**Morning (08:00–12:00)**
- **Theory session (30 min):** How foundations work — why conventional concrete is unnecessary for lightweight timber structures
- **Demonstration:** Earth screw installation with angle grinder
  - Safety: keep hair/clothing clear of spinning socket
  - Technique: vertical alignment, steady pressure, don't force
  - Testing: verify resistance (should not wobble when pulled)
- **Hands-on:** Each participant installs 2–4 earth screws
- **Post installation:** Cut posts to height, install adjustable bases

**Afternoon (13:00–17:00)**
- **Theory session (30 min):** Floor joists and decking — load paths, why 400mm centres
- **Demonstration:** Install perimeter beam and intermediate joists
  - Level with laser level
  - Use joist hangers or direct nailing
  - Double joists at panel junctions
- **Hands-on:** Teams install floor structure
- **Demonstration:** Lay OSB floor decking
  - Stagger joints for strength
  - Glue bead on joists
  - Screw at 150mm centres
  - Leave 3mm expansion gap
- **Hands-on:** Teams lay decking

**Day 1 Learning Check:**
- [ ] Can install an earth screw vertically and safely
- [ ] Can level a timber beam using a laser level
- [ ] Can install floor joists at correct spacing
- [ ] Can lay OSB decking with staggered joints and expansion gaps
- [ ] Understands why the floor structure works structurally

---

### DAY 2 — Wall Panel Fabrication
**Morning (08:00–12:00)**
- **Theory session (45 min):** The RCP panel — how I-joists + pallets + OSB = a composite structural element
  - Why 1.2m × 3.6m is the optimal panel size (two-person lift, pallet dimensions)
  - How glue + nails create a composite action
  - Why 140mm thickness provides insulation and structural depth
- **Demonstration:** Fabricate one complete panel step-by-step
  1. Lay bottom rail (I-joist offcut, 1.2m)
  2. Place 3 vertical I-joists at 400mm centres
  3. Apply construction adhesive to all joints (continuous bead, not spots)
  4. Nail with ring-shank nails (3 per joint, angled for withdrawal resistance)
  5. Check square: measure diagonals, adjust until equal (±5mm)
  6. Staple temporary braces to maintain square during curing
- **Hands-on:** Teams fabricate first panel with instructor supervision

**Afternoon (13:00–17:00)**
- **Hands-on:** Continue panel fabrication (target: 4 panels per team)
- **Quality checks:**
  - Is the panel flat? (no twist >5mm)
  - Are all joints glued and nailed?
  - Are diagonals equal?
- **Pallet slat infill:**
  - Cut slats to fit between I-joists
  - Glue and nail in place
  - Purpose: prevents buckling, adds racking resistance
- **Stacking and storage:** Flat, protected from sun/rain, adhesive curing time

**Day 2 Learning Check:**
- [ ] Can fabricate a complete RCP panel independently
- [ ] Can check and correct panel squareness
- [ ] Understands why glue + nails create composite action
- [ ] Can identify and fix a twisted panel

---

### DAY 3 — Panel Erection
**Morning (08:00–12:00)**
- **Theory session (30 min):** Why panels work as walls — load paths from roof to foundation
- **Demonstration:** Erect first wall panel
  - 4-person lift technique (panel weight: 45kg)
  - Align with floor edge
  - Plumb with spirit level (check both faces)
  - Temporarily brace with 2×4 timber diagonals
  - Fix base to floor joists with L-brackets
- **Hands-on:** Teams erect first panel

**Afternoon (13:00–17:00)**
- **Demonstration:** Connect second panel to first
  - Align panel edge flush
  - Check plumb on both panels
  - Connect with L-brackets (top, middle, bottom)
  - Check corner is square (90° ±1°)
- **Hands-on:** Continue erection until all walls standing
- **Critical checks:**
  - All walls plumb (±5mm per 3m height)
  - All corners square (90° ±1°)
  - Temporary bracing secure against wind
  - No panel leaning or bowing

**Day 3 Learning Check:**
- [ ] Can safely lift and erect a wall panel with a team
- [ ] Can plumb a wall panel using a spirit level
- [ ] Can connect two panels at a corner with L-brackets
- [ ] Can identify and correct an out-of-plumb wall
- [ ] Understands why temporary bracing is essential

---

### DAY 4 — Envelope Completion
**Morning (08:00–12:00)**
- **Theory session (30 min):** Thermal envelope — why weathertightness matters, how insulation works
- **Demonstration:** Install exterior OSB sheathing
  - Glue and screw, same method as panel fabrication
  - Cut openings for windows and door with jigsaw
  - Save cutouts for possible reuse
- **Hands-on:** Teams sheath exterior faces
- **Demonstration:** Install vapour barrier (interior) and breather membrane (exterior)
  - Overlap seams, tape, staple to joists
  - Why vapour barrier goes on the warm side
  - Why breather membrane goes on the cold side

**Afternoon (13:00–17:00)**
- **Demonstration:** Install window and door frames
  - Check opening is square and level
  - Place frame, shim if needed
  - Fix through OSB into I-joist with screws
  - Seal perimeter with expanding foam
- **Hands-on:** Teams install all openings
- **Demonstration:** Install rockwool insulation
  - Cut to fit tightly between I-joists
  - No gaps, no compression
  - Wear dust mask and gloves
- **Hands-on:** Teams insulate all wall cavities

**Day 4 Learning Check:**
- [ ] Can install OSB sheathing on an erected wall
- [ ] Can cut openings accurately with a jigsaw
- [ ] Can install a window frame square and level
- [ ] Can install rockwool insulation without gaps
- [ ] Understands the difference between vapour barrier and breather membrane

---

### DAY 5 — Roof Structure
**Morning (08:00–12:00)**
- **Theory session (30 min):** Roof loads — why the roof is the most stressed element
- **Demonstration:** Install top wall plate
  - Level around perimeter
  - Fix to top of wall panels
  - This is the bearing surface for roof joists
- **Demonstration:** Cut and install roof I-joists
  - Span: building width (5.0m)
  - Spacing: 400mm centres
  - Birdsmouth cuts at wall plate (if pitched roof)
  - Fix with metal joist hangers or direct nailing
- **Hands-on:** Teams install roof joists

**Afternoon (13:00–17:00)**
- **Demonstration:** Install roof OSB decking
  - 18mm thick (or 12mm with 100mm I-joists)
  - Stagger joints, glue and screw
- **Demonstration:** Install roof membrane
  - Roll out EPDM or bituminous membrane
  - Overlap seams 150mm
  - Glue or heat-weld seams
  - Install upstands at edges
- **Hands-on:** Teams complete roof
- **Water-tightness test:** Hose spray for 10 minutes, check for leaks

**Day 5 Learning Check:**
- [ ] Can install a top wall plate level around a perimeter
- [ ] Can cut and install roof joists at correct spacing
- [ ] Can install roof decking with staggered joints
- [ ] Can install a roof membrane with sealed seams
- [ ] Understands why the roof is structurally critical

---

### DAY 6 — Off-Grid Services
**Morning (08:00–12:00)**
- **Theory session (45 min):** Off-grid systems — how solar PV, batteries, and hot water work
  - Solar panels: photovoltaics, DC output, efficiency factors
  - Batteries: lithium chemistry, charge/discharge cycles, capacity
  - Inverter: DC to AC conversion, hybrid operation
  - Solar hot water: flat plate collector, thermosiphon or pump
- **Demonstration:** Install solar PV panels on roof
  - Angle: 30–35° (Algarve latitude)
  - Orientation: South ±15°
  - Fix with brackets to roof structure
- **Hands-on:** Teams install PV array

**Afternoon (13:00–17:00)**
- **Demonstration:** Install battery and inverter
  - Wall-mounted in ventilated area
  - Connect PV input, battery, and distribution board
  - Configure MPPT settings
- **Demonstration:** Install solar hot water panel and tank
  - Roof-mounted flat plate collector
  - 200L tank with electric backup
- **Demonstration:** Install LED lighting (12V circuits)
- **Hands-on:** Teams complete all electrical connections
- **System test:** Check PV output voltage, battery charging, light operation

**Day 6 Learning Check:**
- [ ] Can install solar PV panels at correct angle and orientation
- [ ] Can connect a battery and inverter system
- [ ] Can wire 12V LED lighting circuits
- [ ] Can test and verify system operation
- [ ] Understands basic off-grid system principles

---

### DAY 7 — Interior Finishing
**Morning (08:00–12:00)**
- **Theory session (20 min):** Interior finishes — why natural materials matter, breathability
- **Demonstration:** Install engineered oak flooring
  - Acclimatise boards for 48 hours before laying
  - Click-lock installation, no adhesive
  - Leave 10mm expansion gap at perimeter
- **Hands-on:** Teams lay flooring
- **Demonstration:** Install walnut worktop
  - Cut to length, oil with food-safe oil
  - Install sink and tap

**Afternoon (13:00–17:00)**
- **Demonstration:** Paint interior walls with breathable mineral paint
- **Demonstration:** Install shelving and storage using I-joist offcuts
- **Demonstration:** Install door and window hardware (hinges, handles, locks)
- **Hands-on:** Teams complete interior
- **Quality check:** All surfaces smooth, all hardware operational

**Day 7 Learning Check:**
- [ ] Can install click-lock engineered flooring
- [ ] Can finish a timber worktop with oil
- [ ] Can install basic hardware (hinges, handles)
- [ ] Understands why breathable paints matter in timber construction

---

### DAY 8 — Exterior Finishing and Testing
**Morning (08:00–12:00)**
- **Theory session (20 min):** Timber protection — why pine tar works, maintenance cycles
- **Demonstration:** Apply pine tar to exterior timber
  - Two coats, allow drying between coats
  - All exposed timber: posts, beams, panel edges
- **Hands-on:** Teams treat all exterior timber
- **Demonstration:** Install exterior trim around windows and door
  - Cover panel edges, seal with silicone

**Afternoon (13:00–17:00)**
- **Demonstration:** Install shutters (security and weather protection)
- **Quality control walk-around:**
  - Check all exterior surfaces treated
  - Check all trim installed and sealed
  - Check all joints weatherproof
- **Air-tightness test:** Smoke pencil at joints, look for leaks
- **Thermal imaging scan:** Check insulation continuity (if equipment available)
- **Site clean-up:** Remove waste, organise tools, prepare for handover

**Day 8 Learning Check:**
- [ ] Can apply pine tar correctly for timber protection
- [ ] Can install exterior trim and seal joints
- [ ] Can conduct basic air-tightness testing
- [ ] Understands timber maintenance requirements

---

### DAY 9 — Commissioning and Documentation
**Morning (08:00–12:00)**
- **System commissioning:**
  - Solar PV: verify output, battery charging, inverter display
  - Solar hot water: verify temperature rise
  - LED lighting: all circuits operational
  - Composting toilet: fan operation, correct assembly
  - Windows and door: smooth operation, locking
- **Sensor network installation (if applicable):**
  - Temperature/humidity sensors
  - Energy monitoring
  - Configure data logger

**Afternoon (13:00–17:00)**
- **Final documentation:**
  - As-built drawings: measure and record actual dimensions
  - Photograph every detail
  - Video walkthrough (5–10 minutes)
  - Record any deviations from protocol
- **Team debrief:**
  - What worked well?
  - What was difficult?
  - What would you change?
  - Would you build one for yourself?
- **Certificate presentation:**
  - Each participant receives a certificate of completion
  - Group photo with the completed house

**Day 9 Learning Check:**
- [ ] Can commission and test all building systems
- [ ] Can document a build with photos, video, and measurements
- [ ] Can reflect on lessons learned
- [ ] Has demonstrated all core competencies

---

### DAY 10 — Buffer and Celebration
**Full day for:**
- Any unfinished items from Days 1–9
- Quality corrections
- Additional testing
- Final cleaning and landscaping
- Celebration meal with team
- Planning for 12-month monitoring period

---

## Assessment and Certification

### Competency Checklist
Participants must demonstrate all of the following to receive certification:

- [ ] Fabricate a structural RCP panel that is square and flat
- [ ] Erect a wall panel that is plumb and properly braced
- [ ] Install a window frame square and level with proper sealing
- [ ] Install roof decking and membrane with no leaks
- [ ] Wire and test a 12V LED lighting circuit
- [ ] Install flooring with correct expansion gaps
- [ ] Apply timber protection correctly
- [ ] Complete all safety requirements without incident

### Certificate Text
> **Certificate of Completion**
> 
> This certifies that [Name] has successfully completed the Hestia Foundation 10-Day Self-Build Workshop, demonstrating competency in the fabrication and assembly of the Ribbed Composite Panel (RCP) construction system.
> 
> Date: ________________
> Location: Lagos, Algarve, Portugal
> Instructor: ________________
> 
> [Hestia Foundation seal]

---

## Instructor Requirements

### Lead Instructor
- Completed at least 2 RCP builds
- First aid certified
- Experienced in adult education
- Comfortable with power tools and construction safety

### Assistant Instructors (2–3)
- Completed at least 1 RCP build
- Specialise in: structural, electrical, or finishing
- First aid certified

### Safety Officer
- Dedicated role, not combined with instructing
- Monitors PPE compliance
- Conducts daily safety briefings
- Manages first aid and emergency response

---

## Risk Assessment

| Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation |
|------|-----------|--------|------------|
| Power tool injury | Low | High | Mandatory safety training, PPE, tool guards, supervision |
| Falls from height | Low | High | Fall protection for roof work, no working alone |
| Strain/sprain | Medium | Medium | Warm-up exercises, rotation of tasks, hydration breaks |
| Adhesive/foam exposure | Low | Medium | Ventilation, gloves, eye protection |
| Sun exposure | High | Medium | Shade breaks, sunscreen, hats, hydration |
| Dust inhalation | Medium | Medium | Dust masks mandatory when cutting OSB or insulation |

---

## Materials and Costs (Per Workshop)

| Item | Quantity | Cost (€) |
|------|----------|----------|
| All building materials (Shell A) | 1 set | 25,000 |
| Tools (shared, depreciated) | 1 set | 1,000 |
| Safety equipment | 10 sets | 500 |
| Accommodation (10 nights) | 10 people | 1,500 |
| Meals (10 days) | 10 people | 1,500 |
| Instructor fees (lead + 2 assistants) | 10 days | 3,000 |
| Documentation (cameras, editing) | 1 set | 500 |
| Certificates and materials | 10 | 200 |
| Contingency (10%) | — | 3,300 |
| **TOTAL** | — | **~€37,000** |

**Note:** If the workshop builds the Foundation's prototype, material costs are already budgeted in the prototype build. Workshop-specific costs (accommodation, meals, instructor fees) are additional.

---

## Replication Model

### Option A: Foundation-Hosted Workshop
- Foundation organises and funds
- Participants pay fee (€500–€1,000) or receive scholarship
- Builds Foundation's prototype or demonstration unit
- Best for: generating revenue, building community, media coverage

### Option B: Community-Hosted Workshop
- Community group organises site and participants
- Foundation provides instructor, curriculum, and material list
- Community procures materials locally
- Best for: local ownership, adaptation to local conditions, scalability

### Option C: University-Hosted Workshop
- University incorporates into civil engineering or architecture curriculum
- Students receive academic credit
- Foundation provides build protocol and supervision
- Best for: research integration, student training, academic credibility
